questions.scenario: A script uses this filter: `name contains orders-api`. It finds both staging and production Container Apps. The intended change is staging only. What should the script use before making the change?

options.text: A safer target such as the exact resource ID, expected subscription, resource group, type, and `env=staging` tag check.
The first result returned by Azure, if the script sorts by resource group name and staging usually appears first.
The app name plus region, because the same app rarely has two active environments in one region.
The public hostname from the ticket, because the hostname is the clue responders trust during incidents.

questions.explanation: Partial name matching is risky when environments share names. Exact scope, type, resource ID, and tags make automation safer.

questions.scenario: A release script will restart one Azure resource during a production incident. Several resources share the word `orders`, and the same app exists in dev, staging, and prod. Which guardrails help prevent automation from changing the wrong Azure resource?

options.text: Require the expected subscription and resource group before applying changes.
Compare the full resource ID and resource type against the intended target.
Use tags such as `app`, `env`, and `owner` as additional confirmation.
Allow the script to continue when two resources match, but print both matches in the log for audit.

questions.explanation: Good automation fails closed when the target is ambiguous. Subscription, resource group, type, ID, and tags all reduce the chance of touching the wrong resource.

questions.scenario: A storage account is moved from `rg-devpolaris-orders-temp` to `rg-devpolaris-orders-prod`. The move was approved, but the team has scripts, dashboards, alert rules, and RBAC assignments that may reference the old scope. What should be reviewed after the move?

options.text: Any automation, RBAC, policy, alerts, or documentation that referenced the old resource ID or old resource group.
Only the display name and tags, because a resource keeps its human-facing identity after a move.
Only public endpoint records, because moving resource groups mainly affects how users reach the service.
Only tenant-level settings, because moving between resource groups mostly changes identity ownership.

questions.explanation: Moving a resource can change its resource ID path and surrounding scope. Automation, RBAC, policy, alerts, and docs may need review.

questions.scenario: A policy exemption request names `stdevpolarisordersprod`, but does not include subscription, resource group, or provider type. What should the reviewer ask for?

options.text: The full resource ID and the reason the specific resource needs the exemption.
A portal search link and the resource display name, because reviewers can click through before approving.
A shorter name, because shorter names are safer for policy.
A tag such as `policy-exemption-requested=true`, because it helps reviewers find the resource but does not define scope.

questions.explanation: Policy and RBAC work through scopes and resource identities. A bare name is not enough to approve a scoped exception safely.

questions.scenario: A runbook says, `Rotate secrets for Key Vault orders-prod.` There are two Key Vaults with similar names in different subscriptions. What should be fixed in the runbook?

options.text: Replace the vague name with the exact Key Vault resource ID, subscription, resource group, environment, and owner confirmation.
Keep the vague name but add more prose, because operators can search manually during an incident.
Use the vault with the production-looking tag if the resource group names are too similar during the incident.
Use the vault that the current app revision references, then update the runbook after the rotation.

questions.explanation: Incident runbooks should not rely on guessing. Exact IDs and scope details prevent a stressful manual search from becoming a production mistake.
